summary: @sindresorhus/slugify is a small ESM-only Node.js library (≥ Node 20) that turns arbitrary strings into URL/filename/ID-safe slugs. The implementation lives almost entirely in index.js (~140 lines), with a tiny data file for default symbol replacements, a TypeScript declaration file, and an Ava-based test suite. The main entry exports a default `slugify(string, options?)` plus a stateful `slugifyWithCounter()` that appends `-2`, `-3`, ... to repeated inputs.

findings.claim: Single-purpose string slugifier with broad language support
findings.detail: Package describes itself as 'Slugify a string' and the readme highlights multi-language handling (German umlauts, Vietnamese, Arabic, Russian, etc.).

evidence.quote:
"name": "@sindresorhus/slugify",
	"version": "3.0.0",
	"description": "Slugify a string",

# slugify

> Slugify a string

Useful for URLs, filenames, and IDs.

It handles most major languages, including [German (umlauts)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Germanic_umlaut), Vietnamese, Arabic, Russian, [and more](https://github.com/sindresorhus/transliterate#supported-languages).

sections.title: How to install, run, and start reading

findings.claim: Install as a dependency with npm
findings.detail: Readme documents the standard install command.

evidence.quote:
## Install

```sh
npm install @sindresorhus/slugify
```

findings.claim: `npm test` runs the linter and the Ava test suite
findings.detail: The only script defined is `test`, which runs `xo` and then `ava`. Dev deps confirm both are available.
`type: module` plus an `engines.node` floor of 20 mean callers must use `import` and a modern Node runtime.

findings.claim: Recommended reading order: readme → index.js → tests
findings.detail: `index.js` is the entire implementation (default `slugify` plus `slugifyWithCounter`), and the Ava tests in `test.js` are the most thorough specification of edge cases (possessives, counter behavior, preserveCharacters errors, transliterate:false).

findings.claim: Only three files are published to consumers
findings.detail: `package.json`'s `files` array ships just the implementation, the type declarations, and the small replacements data file.

evidence.quote:
"files": [
		"index.js",
		"index.d.ts",
		"overridable-replacements.js"
	],

findings.claim: Default symbol→word replacements live in a tiny standalone module
findings.detail: `overridable-replacements.js` exports just three pairs (`&`, `🦄`, `♥`) that are merged with user replacements inside `slugify`.

findings.claim: `slugify` pipeline: validate → merge options → optional transliterate + replacements → optional decamelize → build pattern → lowercase → strip possessives → replace disallowed chars → collapse separators → restore preserved affixes
findings.detail: The function flow in `index.js` follows these stages in order; the regex pattern of disallowed characters is built dynamically from the active options.

findings.claim: `decamelize` is a small chain of four regex replacements with special handling for plural acronyms
findings.detail: The final rule excludes `s` (`[a-rt-z]`) so that `APIs` is not split into `API s`. Tests cover this behavior explicitly.

findings.claim: Two runtime dependencies: transliteration and regex escaping
findings.detail: `@sindresorhus/transliterate` provides the Unicode→ASCII mapping and accepts the merged `customReplacements` Map; `escape-string-regexp` is used when injecting user-controlled strings (separator, preserveCharacters) into dynamic regexes.

findings.claim: `transliterate: false` silently disables built-in replacements like `&` → `and`
findings.detail: When `transliterate` is false the code skips the merge with `builtinOverridableReplacements` and only applies the user's `customReplacements`. A test asserts that `'foo & bar'` becomes `'foo-bar'` (not `'foo-and-bar'`) in that mode — a subtle behavioral cliff for callers who toggle transliteration off for performance.

evidence.quote:
// Built-in replacements (like & -> and) are disabled when transliterate is false
	t.is(slugify('foo & bar', {transliterate: false}), 'foo-bar');

findings.detail: `buildPatternSlug` throws an `Error` if any preserved character equals the separator. Callers that build options dynamically must guard against this; tests confirm two throwing cases.

The disallowed-character regex is created with the `g` flag (or `gu`) so a single `.replace` does replace all matches — but the asymmetry vs. nearby `.replaceAll` calls is easy to misread when editing. Any future change that drops the `g` flag from `buildPatternSlug` would silently break slug generation.

evidence.quote:
const flags = options.transliterate ? 'g' : 'gu';
	return new RegExp(`[^${negationSetPattern}]+`, flags);

string = string.replace(patternSlug, options.separator);
	string = string.replaceAll('\\', '');

findings.claim: `slugifyWithCounter` state is per-instance and never auto-resets
findings.detail: Each call to `slugifyWithCounter()` returns its own closure over a `Map`. Sharing one instance across unrelated documents/requests will cause counts to bleed across them; callers must call `.reset()` themselves. Test 'counter' demonstrates how aggressively state accumulates.

findings.claim: Possessive stripping only handles `'s` and `'t` followed by whitespace or end-of-string
findings.detail: The regex requires the suffix character to be exactly `t` or `s` and then `(\s|$)`. Possessives mid-word like `Conway's-Law` (apostrophe followed by `-`) would not be cleaned up the same way, since the lookahead doesn't include the separator. Worth knowing when debugging unexpected hyphenation around quoted text.
